content.text:
```json
[
  {
    "ruleId": "T1",
    "pass": false,
    "severity": "advisory",
    "where": "beat-03 narration, sentence 5-6 ('So say it plainly... one honest reading of alignment comes out')",
    "evidence": "beat-03 calls the dot product 'one honest reading of alignment' right after beat-02 already demonstrated that a stretched candidate's score climbs on length alone with no change in alignment (\"a long arrow can fake a high number\"); calling the reading 'honest' here sits oddly against a beat later explicitly titled 'The honest look' (beat-04b), which exists specifically to complicate this claim",
    "suggestion": "hedge the claim here, e.g. 'one reading of alignment comes out — as long as length stays fixed' — so 'honest' isn't spent before the beat that earns the word"
  },
  {
    "ruleId": "T2",
    "pass": false,
    "severity": "advisory",
    "where": "page section 'What did that sweep actually teach you?', sentence 'Before you scroll on: positive, zero, negative. Say what each reading claims about two vectors, in your own words.'",
    "evidence": "the page has used 'arrow' consistently throughout (probe arrow, candidate arrows, taste arrow) and never uses the word 'vector' anywhere before this sentence; 'vectors' appears here unglossed",
    "suggestion": "say 'two arrows' to match the vocabulary the page has built, or add a one-clause aside the first time 'vector' appears ('vector — the arrow you've been turning')"
  },
  {
    "ruleId": "T3",
    "pass": false,
    "severity": "advisory",
    "where": "beat-05 narration, sentence 'One more aisle, to make sure it lands: score yourself against a job posting.'",
    "evidence": "'one more aisle' presumes a first aisle, but the video never used the word 'aisle' before this point — the catalog was called a 'shelf' in beat-04 ('The catalog is a shelf of candidates'), not an aisle",
    "suggestion": "either introduce 'aisle' earlier (e.g. pair it with 'shelf' in beat-04) or drop the implied callback: 'try it in one more setting: score yourself against a job posting'"
  }
]
```
